claims.claim: For individuals 2 years and older who do not consume seafood, the regular intake of other foods high in omega-3 fatty acids, such as eggs that contain omega-3 fatty acids, is appropriate.
claims.importance_rationale: This provides a specific recommendation for eggs as a source of omega-3 fatty acids for those avoiding seafood, which is relevant to cardiovascular health.
claims.relevance_rationale: This provides a specific recommendation for eggs as a source of omega-3 fatty acids for those avoiding seafood, which is relevant to cardiovascular health.
claims.source_quote: For those who do not consume seafood, the Committee concluded that regular intake of other foods high in omega-3 fatty acids, such as flaxseeds, walnuts, soy oil, algae, and eggs that contain omega-3 fatty acids, is appropriate.

claim_context.evidence_design: Committee review of evidence on types of dietary fats and cardiovascular disease risk
claim_context.exposure_or_option: Regular intake of eggs containing omega-3 fatty acids
claim_context.outcome_or_endpoint: Appropriate dietary pattern for those avoiding seafood
claim_context.population: Individuals 2 years and older who do not consume seafood

must_preserve_terms: eggs that contain omega-3 fatty acids
whole_doc_source_card.natural_bottom_line: Eggs containing omega-3 fatty acids are an appropriate substitute for those who do not eat seafood.
whole_doc_source_card.source_bottom_line: The 2020-2025 Dietary Guidelines recommend a healthy dietary pattern including various protein sources, specifically noting that for individuals who do not consume seafood, regular intake of eggs containing omega-3 fatty acids is appropriate.

supporting_quotes.quote: For those who do not consume seafood, the Committee concluded that regular intake of other foods high in omega-3 fatty acids, such as flaxseeds, walnuts, soy oil, algae, and eggs that contain omega-3 fatty acids, is appropriate.

claims.claim: The Dietary Guidelines recommend including eggs as part of a variety of animal-source foods (meat, poultry, seafood, eggs, and dairy) for infants and toddlers beginning at 6 to 12 months old.

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Recommended starting age for complementary foods

claims.importance_rationale: Establishes the baseline recommendation for egg consumption in early life stages.
claims.relevance_rationale: Establishes the baseline recommendation for egg consumption in early life stages.
claims.source_quote: The Committee recommended providing infants and toddlers with a variety of animal-source foods (meat, poultry, seafood, eggs, and dairy), fruits and vegetables, nuts and seeds, and whole-grain products, beginning at 6 to 12 months old and continuing thereafter.

claim_context.evidence_design: Food pattern modeling and evidence review
claim_context.exposure_or_option: Variety of animal-source foods (meat, poultry, seafood, eggs, and dairy)
claim_context.outcome_or_endpoint: Nutritional adequacy and allergy risk reduction

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Recommended starting age for complementary foods

whole_doc_source_card.natural_bottom_line: Eggs should be introduced to infants and toddlers starting at 6 months of age as part of a varied diet.
whole_doc_source_card.source_bottom_line: The 2020-2025 Dietary Guidelines recommend a healthy dietary pattern including various protein sources, specifically noting that for individuals who do not consume seafood, regular intake of eggs containing omega-3 fatty acids is appropriate.

supporting_quotes.quote: The Committee recommended providing infants and toddlers with a variety of animal-source foods (meat, poultry, seafood, eggs, and dairy), fruits and vegetables, nuts and seeds, and whole-grain products, beginning at 6 to 12 months old and continuing thereafter.

claims.claim: Adding peanut and egg in developmentally appropriate forms in the first year of life (6-12 months old) may reduce the risk of food allergy to these foods.

claim_quantities.source_quote: in the first year of life (6-12 months old)

claims.importance_rationale: Provides a safety/allergy context for early egg introduction.
claims.relevance_rationale: Provides a safety/allergy context for early egg introduction.
claims.source_quote: The Committee concluded that, once CFB are introduced, adding peanut and egg in developmentally appropriate forms in the first year of life (6-12 months old) may reduce the risk of food allergy to these foods.

claim_context.evidence_design: Committee conclusion based on evidence review
claim_context.exposure_or_option: Peanut and egg in developmentally appropriate forms

claim_quantities.source_quote: in the first year of life (6-12 months old)
